spec: Rank cat pictures by the authenticity of feline behavior captured in each image. Evaluate whether each cat is engaged in genuine, natural behavior arising from its own instincts and impulses — kneading, leaping, sleeping in absurd positions, stalking, startling, grooming, or simply existing in that particular unselfconscious way cats do. Images where the cat is clearly being itself, doing what it would do whether or not anyone was watching, should rank highest. Images where the cat appears posed, costumed, propped up, held in position, or otherwise arranged for the camera should rank lowest. The critical distinction is between a cat expressing its own nature and a cat being used as a prop or vehicle for human cleverness. A cat in a costume held up to mimic a human pose is contrived. A cat caught mid-leap with legs splayed in improbable directions is authentic. A cat kneading a blanket with half-closed eyes lost in private contentment is authentic. Focus exclusively on the naturalness and genuineness of the cat's behavior — whether it feels real, instinctive, and unperformed — not on image quality, composition, or any other photographic consideration.

Score this cat picture for the emotional resonance of the captured moment. Evaluate whether the image produces a genuine emotional response — delight, tenderness, laughter, warmth — that arises naturally and inseparably from the moment itself, rather than from artificial packaging layered on top. Consider whether the image would still move you if all captions, filters, overlaid text, or framing devices were stripped away. An image where the feeling lives in the moment — the curl of a sleeping cat's paw, the wide-eyed alarm of a startled kitten, the gentle press of a cat leaning into a human hand — should score highly. An image that relies on external embellishments to manufacture a reaction it does not inherently possess should score low. The key question is whether the emotional power of this picture comes from what is actually happening in it or from how it has been dressed up. Score based on how much genuine, earned emotional power lives within the captured moment itself. A high score means the moment is inherently affecting. A low score means the emotion is shallow, manufactured, or dependent on packaging rather than content.

Rank cat pictures by the quality of witnessing — whether each image feels like a moment that was discovered and caught rather than deliberately produced. Look for the texture of accident, spontaneity, and fortunate timing: signs that someone was simply present, noticed something worth preserving, and captured it. Imperfect framing, natural lighting, the ordinary clutter of a real lived-in environment, and the palpable sense of a fleeting instant frozen in time all indicate high witnessing quality. Images that feel like small, generous acts of sharing — someone saying 'look what I was lucky enough to see' — should rank highest. Images that announce their own production through deliberate staging, controlled backdrops, professional lighting setups, careful composition, or the unmistakable air of a planned photoshoot should rank lowest. The quality of witnessing also encompasses timing — the sense that the image captures a fleeting instant that will not come again. A cat mid-yawn, a kitten experiencing something for the first time, a cat frozen in an absurd posture for a split second — these derive power from their impermanence. Focus on whether the image feels found or made, discovered or manufactured, witnessed or produced.
